title: Undefined behavior, tests and sanitizers

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Undefined behavior, tests and sanitizers; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: AddressSanitizer catches an exercised out-of-bounds write, and the fix is justified by array bounds rather than by merely silencing the tool. Your goal is to find and repair bounds, lifetime, overflow and uninitialized-state defects using tests and sanitizers. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Failure-safe cleanup; Scope, linkage and storage duration; Multi-file builds. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Undefined behavior has no required result, so prevention uses standard reasoning, strict diagnostics, targeted tests, and sanitizers that detect only the paths and classes they instrument. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Sanitizers are not the language specification, miss unexecuted paths, can change timing or layout, and different tools cover different undefined or memory behaviors. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: If sanitizer tests pass, the C program contains no undefined behavior on any input.

includes: Distinguish defined, implementation-defined and undefined behavior and combine tests with dynamic diagnostics.
Find and repair bounds, lifetime, overflow and uninitialized-state defects using tests and sanitizers.
excludes: Sanitizers are not the language specification, miss unexecuted paths, can change timing or layout, and different tools cover different undefined or memory behaviors.

outcomes: Find and repair bounds, lifetime, overflow and uninitialized-state defects using tests and sanitizers.

examples.title: Undefined behavior, tests and sanitizers: defined case
examples.situation: AddressSanitizer catches an exercised out-of-bounds write, and the fix is justified by array bounds rather than by merely silencing the tool.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: AddressSanitizer catches an exercised out-of-bounds write, and the fix is justified by array bounds rather than by merely silencing the tool.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Sanitizer success is path-limited evidence; combine it with contracts, bounds and lifetime reasoning, diagnostics, and broader tests.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Sanitizer success is path-limited evidence; combine it with contracts, bounds and lifetime reasoning, diagnostics, and broader tests.

examples.title: Undefined behavior, tests and sanitizers: boundary case
examples.situation: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer reports signed overflow in a test, prompting a range check or unsigned design before the operation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer reports signed overflow in a test, prompting a range check or unsigned design before the operation.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Sanitizers are not the language specification, miss unexecuted paths, can change timing or layout, and different tools cover different undefined or memory behaviors.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: If sanitizer tests pass, the C program contains no undefined behavior on any input.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Find and repair bounds, lifetime, overflow and uninitialized-state defects using tests and sanitizers.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If sanitizer tests pass, the C program contains no undefined behavior on any input.
misconceptions.correction: Sanitizer success is path-limited evidence; combine it with contracts, bounds and lifetime reasoning, diagnostics, and broader tests.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: If sanitizer tests pass, the C program contains no undefined behavior on any input.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Undefined behavior, tests and sanitizers: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Sanitizers are not the language specification, miss unexecuted paths, can change timing or layout, and different tools cover different undefined or memory behaviors.

recap.summary: You should now be able to find and repair bounds, lifetime, overflow and uninitialized-state defects using tests and sanitizers. Keep this boundary in view: Sanitizers are not the language specification, miss unexecuted paths, can change timing or layout, and different tools cover different undefined or memory behaviors.
